(1 April 1943 – 16 September 1991) was an English actress.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British actress (1943–1991)

For the philosopher, see [Carol J. White](/wiki/Carol_J._White "Carol J. White"). For the model, see [Carole White](/wiki/Carole_White "Carole White").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Carol White"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Carol+White%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Carol+White%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Carol+White%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Carol+White%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Carol+White%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Carol+White%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(October 2010)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

| Born | Carole Joan White  (1943-04-01)1 April 1943  [Hammersmith](/wiki/Hammersmith "Hammersmith"), [London](/wiki/London "London"), [England](/wiki/England "England"), UK[[1]](#cite_note-Meade-1) |
| Died | 16 September 1991(1991-09-16) (aged 48)  [Miami, Florida](/wiki/Miami,_Florida "Miami, Florida"), U.S. |

**Carole Joan White** (1 April 1943 – 16 September 1991) was an English actress.

White became famous for her performances in the television play *[Cathy Come Home](/wiki/Cathy_Come_Home "Cathy Come Home")* (1966) and the films *[Poor Cow](/wiki/Poor_Cow "Poor Cow")* (1967) and *[I'll Never Forget What's 'isname](/wiki/I%27ll_Never_Forget_What%27s%27isname "I'll Never Forget What's'isname")* (1967), and by the end of the 1960s was hailed as 'the next [Julie Christie](/wiki/Julie_Christie "Julie Christie")'.[[2]](#cite_note-Reed_Conversations-2) [Alcoholism](/wiki/Alcoholism "Alcoholism") and [drug abuse](/wiki/Drug_abuse "Drug abuse") damaged her career, however, and from the early 1970s she worked infrequently.

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carol_White&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

White, the daughter of a [scrap merchant](/wiki/Scrap_merchant "Scrap merchant"), was born in [Hammersmith](/wiki/Hammersmith "Hammersmith"), London on 1 April 1943. She attended the [Corona Stage Academy](/wiki/Corona_Stage_Academy "Corona Stage Academy").[[1]](#cite_note-Meade-1)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carol_White&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

White played minor parts in films from 1949 until the late 1950s, when she began to play more substantial supporting roles in films such as *[Carry On Teacher](/wiki/Carry_On_Teacher "Carry On Teacher")* (1959) and *[Never Let Go](/wiki/Never_Let_Go_(1960_film) "Never Let Go (1960 film)")* (1960), in which she played the girlfriend of [Peter Sellers](/wiki/Peter_Sellers "Peter Sellers"). She also acted the part of Evelyn May, a 'girl in the bar' and court witness in [Sidney J. Furie](/wiki/Sidney_J._Furie "Sidney J. Furie")'s *[The Boys](/wiki/The_Boys_(1962_British_film) "The Boys (1962 British film)")* (1962).

After marrying, she became disenchanted with acting due to the quality of her roles. She moved back to her [home borough](/wiki/London_borough "London borough") of [Hammersmith](/wiki/Hammersmith_and_Fulham "Hammersmith and Fulham") to settle down to married life.[[2]](#cite_note-Reed_Conversations-2) Growing bored with it, White began to audition for roles again and was cast in the television version of [Nell Dunn](/wiki/Nell_Dunn "Nell Dunn")'s *[Up the Junction](/wiki/Up_the_Junction_(The_Wednesday_Play) "Up the Junction (The Wednesday Play)")* (1965) by director [Ken Loach](/wiki/Ken_Loach "Ken Loach").[[2]](#cite_note-Reed_Conversations-2) She followed this up with two other [Wednesday Plays](/wiki/Wednesday_Play "Wednesday Play") for Loach, *The Coming Out Party* (1965) and *[Cathy Come Home](/wiki/Cathy_Come_Home "Cathy Come Home")* (1966), which made her a TV star in the UK.[[1]](#cite_note-Meade-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Reed_Conversations-2)

She followed this success with the lead in Loach's film *[Poor Cow](/wiki/Poor_Cow "Poor Cow")* (1967), based on another Nell Dunn book, which brought her international renown.[[2]](#cite_note-Reed_Conversations-2)[[3]](#cite_note-three-3) She also appeared in *[I'll Never Forget What's'isname](/wiki/I%27ll_Never_Forget_What%27s%27isname "I'll Never Forget What's'isname")* with Oliver Reed in 1967, by which time her career was approaching its apogee.[[1]](#cite_note-Meade-1) White followed this up by co-starring opposite [Alan Bates](/wiki/Alan_Bates "Alan Bates") and [Dirk Bogarde](/wiki/Dirk_Bogarde "Dirk Bogarde") in [John Frankenheimer](/wiki/John_Frankenheimer "John Frankenheimer")'s film adaptation of [Bernard Malamud](/wiki/Bernard_Malamud "Bernard Malamud")'s *[The Fixer](/wiki/The_Fixer_(1968_film) "The Fixer (1968 film)")* (1968), which was a critical success.[[1]](#cite_note-Meade-1)

Subsequently, she travelled to Hollywood in 1968 to make *[Daddy's Gone A-Hunting](/wiki/Daddy%27s_Gone_A-Hunting_(1969_film) "Daddy's Gone A-Hunting (1969 film)")* (1969).[[1]](#cite_note-Meade-1) Her career started to cool off by the time she appeared in [Andrew V. McLaglen](/wiki/Andrew_V._McLaglen "Andrew V. McLaglen")'s [western](/wiki/Western_film "Western film") [comedy](/wiki/Comedy_film "Comedy film"), *[Something Big](/wiki/Something_Big_(film) "Something Big (film)")* (1971), which starred [Dean Martin](/wiki/Dean_Martin "Dean Martin"). Back in the UK, she had major roles in *[Dulcima](/wiki/Dulcima "Dulcima")*, alongside [John Mills](/wiki/John_Mills "John Mills") (1971),[[1]](#cite_note-Meade-1) and *[Made](/wiki/Made_(1972_film) "Made (1972 film)")* (1972), with the singer [Roy Harper](/wiki/Roy_Harper_(singer) "Roy Harper (singer)").

During the late 1960s, White was considered one of the most promising actresses in British cinema, but her alcoholism and substance abuse,[[1]](#cite_note-Meade-1) as well as unhappy relationships with male stars such as [Richard Burton](/wiki/Richard_Burton "Richard Burton"), [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra"), [Oliver Reed](/wiki/Oliver_Reed "Oliver Reed") and [Paul Burke](/wiki/Paul_Burke_(actor) "Paul Burke (actor)"), hindered her career.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] She did, however, have a prominent role as a hostage in *[The Squeeze](/wiki/The_Squeeze_(1977_film) "The Squeeze (1977 film)")* (1977).

After living in Hollywood for several years, White returned to London to star in Nell Dunn's play *[Steaming](/wiki/Steaming_(play) "Steaming (play)")*[[1]](#cite_note-Meade-1) at the [Comedy Theatre](/wiki/Comedy_Theatre "Comedy Theatre") in the West End, and filmed *[Nutcracker](/wiki/Nutcracker_(film) "Nutcracker (film)")* at the same time.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Despite receiving excellent reviews for *Steaming*, she was often late, or else missed performances altogether, and was finally sacked.[[1]](#cite_note-Meade-1)

In 1982, a biography, *Carol Comes Home* by [Clifford Thurlow](/wiki/Clifford_Thurlow "Clifford Thurlow"), was published.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Although White received plenty of publicity for both the play and the biography, she was unable to revive her career. She returned to the United States, where she lived the rest of her life.

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carol_White&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

White dated [Terence Stamp](/wiki/Terence_Stamp "Terence Stamp"), who also had been involved with Julie Christie, the actress she was most compared to. Stamp introduced her to [Lionel Bart](/wiki/Lionel_Bart "Lionel Bart"), who introduced her to her future husband Michael King, of [The King Brothers](/wiki/The_King_Brothers "The King Brothers") pop group. They settled down in Hammersmith, where she had been born, and raised their two children, Sean and Stephen.

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carol_White&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Death")]

White died in 1991 in [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"), at the age of 48. The cause of her death is disputed, with some sources claiming she took a drug overdose, and others (*[The Sunday Times](/wiki/The_Sunday_Times "The Sunday Times")* in 1991 and Julian Upton, writing in 2004)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Upton-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) suggesting she succumbed to liver disease,[[9]](#cite_note-9) brought on by chronic alcoholism.

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carol_White&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Legacy")]

A television film of her life, *The Battersea Bardot*, was shown in 1994, with White portrayed by [Wendy Morgan](/wiki/Wendy_Morgan_(actress) "Wendy Morgan (actress)").

## Filmography
